facts.fact: PostgreSQL WAL follows the principle that changes must be written to the append-only log and flushed to disk before changes are applied to data pages. If the system crashes during change application, the WAL can be replayed to restore consistency.

PostgreSQL crash recovery identifies the REDO point from the most recent checkpoint and replays XLOG records forward. Before replaying, PostgreSQL checks if the XLOG record's LSN is greater than the page's LSN—if so, the change hasn't been applied yet.

PostgreSQL uses Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC) where every UPDATE or DELETE doesn't immediately remove the old row—instead it's marked as dead and remains in the table until VACUUM cleans it up.

Long-running transactions block VACUUM from cleaning dead tuples because VACUUM must guarantee that those tuples 'cannot possibly be referenced by any currently running transaction.' One forgotten BEGIN without COMMIT can bloat an entire database.

PostgreSQL's autovacuum uses the formula: Dead Tuples > (autovacuum_vacuum_threshold + autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor * Total Tuples). Default threshold is 50 tuples with scale_factor of 0.2 (20% of table).

The ARIES crash recovery algorithm has three phases: (1) Analysis reads the log from last checkpoint to identify dirty pages and active transactions; (2) Redo replays all changes forward; (3) Undo reverses uncommitted transactions. Each log record includes a Log Sequence Number (LSN).

PostgreSQL transaction IDs (XIDs) are 32-bit unsigned integers (0 to 4,294,967,295). When approaching the maximum, PostgreSQL stops WRITE operations and switches to READ-ONLY mode if there are 2,000,000,000 unvacuumed transactions to prevent wraparound.

PostgreSQL SERIALIZABLE isolation level uses Serializable Snapshot Isolation (SSI) to detect and prevent serialization anomalies. Only updating transactions need retry; read-only transactions never have serialization conflicts.

B-Tree indexes are the default and support equality and range queries (=, <, >, <=, >=, BETWEEN, IN). Hash indexes support only equality (=) and cannot handle ranges, sorting, or IS NULL. GiST indexes use a flexible framework for spatial data and overlapping predicates.

PostgreSQL 14 introduced bottom-up index tuple deletion, which deletes index entries pointing to dead tuples just before an index page split occurs. This prevents expensive page splits and reduces subsequent bloat that would occur when VACUUM cleans up.
WAL segment files are normally 16 MB each. The wal_sync_method parameter determines how PostgreSQL asks the kernel to force WAL updates to disk (fsync, fdatasync, fsync_writethrough, open_datasync).

PostgreSQL's asynchronous commit (synchronous_commit = off) returns success immediately without waiting for WAL to flush to disk, providing significant throughput boost for small transactions but risking data loss on crash (not corruption).

READ COMMITTED isolation level sees a snapshot as of the start of the current statement. REPEATABLE READ sees a snapshot as of the first non-transaction-control statement. PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ doesn't allow phantom reads (stronger than SQL standard).

pg_stat_activity shows one row per server process with details of running queries. pg_blocking_pids(pid) function returns PIDs that are blocking the specified process. Combined with pg_locks, this enables full lock conflict analysis.
Heap-Only Tuples (HOT) updates avoid unnecessary index updates when non-indexed columns are modified, reducing index bloat, disk I/O, and frequency of vacuuming and reindexing.
PostgreSQL 13+ deduplication occurs lazily when a new item insertion cannot fit on an existing B-tree leaf page, preventing or delaying page splits and reducing the cascade of bloat.

examples.incident: Customer experienced massive IOPS spikes caused by table bloat with tens of millions of dead tuples. After increasing autovacuum_max_workers to 8 and autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit to 4800, Read IOPS climbed from baseline to 10,000 sustained.

In May 2021, PostgreSQL cluster hit the XID wraparound problem—long-running transactions held back the xmin horizon so severely that new transactions could not be assigned new XIDs. This is a critical availability event requiring manual recovery.

examples.company: Generic high-churn OLTP workload (real observed pattern)
examples.incident: A table with 100 million rows updated 5 million times per day with default autovacuum settings (scale_factor = 0.2). Autovacuum doesn't trigger until 20 million dead tuples accumulate, causing sequential scans to slow by 3-5x as the table bloats and requires scanning more disk pages.

A read replica lagged behind primary recovery due to slow WAL replay on a table with heavily fragmented B-tree indexes. After autovacuum didn't keep up with deletes, page splits cascaded upwards, doubling index size.

Forgot to close a reporting query transaction for 8 hours, blocking all VACUUM operations. Dead tuples accumulated to 500 million rows in the main transactional table, dropping query performance by 60% until the forgotten transaction was terminated.

analogies.analogy: Write-Ahead Logging is like writing to a captain's log before executing orders. If the ship is destroyed mid-order, the log survives and can be used to replay what was done and what still needs to happen.

MVCC is like a library where every book has multiple versions, and each reader checks out the version that was current when they arrived. Readers never block writers because writers just add new versions; old versions stay until no reader needs them anymore.

Table bloat from autovacuum lag is like a desk where you cross out items instead of erasing them. After months of updates, your desk is full of crossed-out items. Vacuum is like rewriting a clean desk, but if you keep crossing out items faster than you can rewrite, your desk just gets fuller.

B-tree index page splits are like a filing cabinet drawer that's full. To add a new file, you split the drawer in two, which is expensive. Bottom-up deletion prevents splits by removing items just before overflow, like archiving old files before they force a split.

Long-running transactions are like a customer who entered a store 6 hours ago and is still shopping—the manager can't close the store or clean up until that customer leaves, blocking all cleanup for everyone else.

XID wraparound is like a car odometer that goes from 9,999 back to 0,000. If the car keeps running without service (vacuum), it hits a critical failure state before looping. PostgreSQL forbids writes to prevent corruption.

key_numbers.context: Vacuum triggers when: dead_tuples > 50 + (0.2 * total_tuples). For a 10M row table, triggers at ~2M dead tuples.
VACUUM doesn't trigger until 20% of table rows are dead. For large tables, this is too late and causes bloat.
For a 60M row table with 2% scale_factor, vacuum triggers at ~1.2M dead tuples instead of 12M, preventing severe bloat.
Each WAL file is 16MB. Recovery time scales with WAL size between checkpoints.
PostgreSQL stops WRITE operations when 2 billion unvacuumed transactions remain, leaving 2B XIDs before wraparound failure.
Frequent checkpoints reduce recovery time after unclean shutdown and spread I/O more evenly.
PostgreSQL completes each checkpoint in ~50% of the time before the next one starts. Higher values (0.9) reduce I/O spikes.
Real AWS RDS case: increasing autovacuum_max_workers to 8 and cost_limit to 4800 brought Read IOPS to 10,000 sustained on bloated tables.

key_numbers.metric: Query performance degradation from table bloat
key_numbers.context: Sequential scans on severely bloated tables degrade by 3-5x as they must scan more disk pages to fetch the same logical rows.
PostgreSQL logs when a transaction waits >1s for a lock, controlled by deadlock_timeout parameter.

common_misconceptions.misconception: VACUUM deletes rows immediately when DELETE is called.
common_misconceptions.reality: DELETE just marks rows as dead. VACUUM later identifies and removes dead tuples. Long-running transactions can prevent VACUUM from cleaning, causing unbounded bloat.

common_misconceptions.misconception: READ COMMITTED isolation level allows dirty reads.
common_misconceptions.reality: READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads. It sees a snapshot as of the start of each statement. The main issue is non-repeatable reads between statements in the same transaction.

common_misconceptions.misconception: REPEATABLE READ allows phantom reads (new rows appearing mid-transaction).
common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ prevents phantom reads—stronger than the SQL standard requires. It sees a consistent snapshot of all existing rows from the start of the transaction.

common_misconceptions.misconception: B-tree indexes automatically defragment and stay compact.
common_misconceptions.reality: B-tree index bloat persists even after VACUUM. Page splits don't reclaim space; they split pages into two bloated pages. PostgreSQL 14+ bottom-up deletion helps, but old indexes can require REINDEX.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash indexes are faster than B-tree for all equality queries.
common_misconceptions.reality: Hash indexes are only slightly faster for single equality checks and cannot handle ranges, sorting, or IS NULL. B-tree is the right default for nearly all workloads.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Turning off fsync is equivalent to disabling synchronous_commit.
common_misconceptions.reality: Disabling synchronous_commit provides most WAL performance benefits without data corruption risk. Turning off fsync risks corruption if the OS crashes during write buffering.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Autovacuum will always keep tables bloat-free with default settings.
common_misconceptions.reality: Default settings (20% scale factor, threshold 50) are too lenient for high-churn tables. Autovacuum can fall behind by 10-100x on tables with millions of updates per day.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Forgotten BEGIN without COMMIT just leaves a transaction open harmlessly.
common_misconceptions.reality: A forgotten transaction blocks all VACUUM operations and prevents garbage collection of dead tuples, causing unbounded bloat and potential XID wraparound crisis.

common_misconceptions.misconception: WAL replay is instant because it's sequential I/O.
common_misconceptions.reality: WAL replay can be slow (hours on large databases with sparse updates) because each record is applied individually and may require random page access. Parallel recovery (PostgreSQL 13+) helps.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Index bloat doesn't matter if queries are fast.
common_misconceptions.reality: Index bloat increases memory pressure, reduces cache efficiency, and slows sequential index scans. It also increases replication lag and recovery time.

common_misconceptions.misconception: SERIALIZABLE isolation level is the default and always safe.
common_misconceptions.reality: The default is READ COMMITTED. SERIALIZABLE requires retry logic and can cause high contention in write-heavy workloads.
pg_blocking_pids() shows only direct blockers, not transitive lock chains. Complex deadlock scenarios may require joining pg_locks with pg_stat_activity to fully understand the graph.
